stage1.why: Stephen King wrote no book titled 'Panic'; novels of that title are by authors such as Lauren Oliver and Jeff Abbott.
Break Up (1998) was directed by Paul Marcus, and The Break-Up (2006) by Peyton Reed; Michael Bay directed neither.
BWV 63 is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
Hitchcock never directed a film called The Suspect; the 1944 film was directed by Robert Siodmak (2013 versions by Won Shin-yun / Stuart Connelly).
No film titled The Dove was written by Steinbeck; the 1974 film was scripted by Peter S. Beagle and Adam Kennedy, the 1927 film adapted from Willard Mack's play.
Cannot identify which work 'The Copper' refers to, so its catalogued genre is undeterminable.
The question does not identify which 'Morgan' is meant, so the intended father cannot be determined.
The 2007 documentary was directed by Rene Daalder and produced by Aaron Ohlmann; John Malkovich was not its producer.
Notable athletes named Atanas Atanasov are footballers, a volleyball player, a basketball player, and a long jumper — none is a tennis player.
The notable Vladimir Korotkov (Soviet tennis player, 1948-2025) was born in Moscow per Wikipedia/ATP.
Hitchcock's fully documented filmography contains no film titled The Victor.
Tadhg Dall Ó hUiginn was a 16th-century Irish bardic poet, not a teacher.
The American landscape painter Robert Crannell Minor was born in New York City in 1839.
KIQZ is a radio station in Rawlins, Wyoming, United States.
Basin City is a community in Franklin County, Washington, United States.
He was born in Ubud (per Wikidata); Bali is the island/province, not the city, so the answer names the wrong entity.
"Mr. Thopia" is a placeholder that identifies no one; Niketa Thopia's father was Karl Topia.
Multiple sportsmen named Paul Hoffman(n) exist (NBA basketball player, Scotland cricketer); no prominent footballer of that name is verifiable, but the intended referent is indeterminate.
Mohd Sharkar Shamsudin is a Malaysian politician (Pahang), not a teacher.
Bruckheimer has no film titled Contract; e.g., Contract (2008) was produced by Ram Gopal Varma and The Contract (2006) by other producers.
David Heyman's filmography contains no film titled 'Home'; known 'Home' films (2008, 2009, 2013, 2015) have other producers.
The most plausible referents (the 1967 film Hotel and the 1983 TV series Hotel) are both dramas.
Obeakpu is a village/community in Imo State, Nigeria.
John Hughes's well-documented filmography contains no film titled 'The Right Way'; the films of that title were written by others.
Kanjiroba Himal is a mountain in the Dolpa district of Nepal, not Japan.
Joseph MacRory was a Catholic cardinal and Archbishop of Armagh.
'Alt' is ambiguous (band ALT, several albums); cannot determine the referent or its catalogued genre with confidence.
Revigliasco d'Asti is a comune in the Province of Asti, Piedmont, Italy.
Kandathum Kettathum (1988) was directed by Balachandra Menon, not Kamal (verified via Wikipedia/IMDb).
The notable places named Rogers (e.g. Rogers, Arkansas; Rogers, Minnesota) are in the United States, not Canada.
Long Pants (1927, dir. Frank Capra) was produced by Harry Langdon via the Harry Langdon Corporation; John Hughes (b. 1950) is wrong.
Neither the 2008 French film Disco (written by Fabien Onteniente et al.) nor the 2019 Norwegian film (Jorunn Myklebust Syversen) was written by John Hughes.
Imperatriz Airport is in Imperatriz, Maranhão, Brazil.
Konjsko Brdo is a village in the Perušić municipality, Lika-Senj County, Croatia.
The most prominent Museum of Islamic Art is in Doha, Qatar.
Guy (Gui) of Amiens was a son of Enguerrand I of Ponthieu's line, not of Saint Bernard.
Princess Yamanobe (7th century, wife of Prince Otsu) was a daughter of Emperor Tenji, not Emperor Meiji (per Wikipedia/Wikidata Q3138027).
Jean Dorion, the Quebec politician (b. 1943), was born in Montreal.
Multiple works are titled 'The Children' (horror film, Wharton novel, Kirkwood play) with different genres; the intended referent is indeterminate.
Kirk O'Bee, the American cyclist, was born in Ann Arbor, Michigan, not New York.
The Black Book (1949, aka Reign of Terror) was directed by Anthony Mann; the 2006 Black Book (Zwartboek) by Paul Verhoeven — neither is Lars von Trier.
The most plausible referent, the 1978 film Northern Lights, was produced by John Hanson and Rob Nilsson (per Wikipedia/IMDb); no notable 'Northern Lights' was produced by David Attenborough.
Multiple works are titled 'Sly' (Wolf-Ferrari opera, 2018 Iranian comedy, 2023 documentary); the intended referent and its genre cannot be determined.
Beethoven composed a Symphony No. 3 (the Eroica), the most plausible referent of the underspecified question.
No version of The Spoilers (1914-1955, from Rex Beach's novel) was written by John Ford; e.g. the 1942 film was scripted by Lawrence Hazard and Tom Reed.
FK (fudbalski klub) Mladost Bački Jarak is a Serbian association football club.
Jeannot Gilbert was a Canadian professional ice hockey player (Boston Bruins), not a rugby player.
J.K. Rowling has no work titled 'Anniversary'; known works of that title are by other authors.
The song 'Estate' was composed by Bruno Martino (1960); John Williams has no known work titled 'Estate'.
Spielberg directed no film titled Tomorrow; e.g. the 1972 film Tomorrow was directed by Joseph Anthony.
Sune Sik was reputedly a son of King Sverker I of Sweden, not the god Odin.
Robert Frost has no work titled 'Images'; that title belongs to other authors (e.g., Richard Aldington's imagist collection), not Frost.
Matteo Pivotto is an Italian footballer (Serie A/B defender), not a tennis player.
Žarko Petan was a Slovenian writer, aphorist, and theatre director, not a teacher.
The musical Sunny (1925) was composed by Jerome Kern, and the song Sunny is by Bobby Hebb — not George Gershwin.
The Dub (1919) was directed by James Cruze; John Hughes directed no such film.
No film 'The Lamp' directed by John R. Miller exists; known versions were directed by Tom Daley (1987) and Tracy Trost (2011).
Competitors is a 2008 novel by Sergey Lukyanenko; Grisham has no work by that title.
Andreas Rüdiger (1673-1731) was a German philosopher and physician who practiced medicine and lectured on philosophy; he was not a professor of physics.
The 2019 film Glass was written (and directed) by M. Night Shyamalan.
John Hughes has no film titled 'Threat' in his producing credits; the indie film Threat (2006) was produced by Matt Pizzolo and Katie Nisa.
A London Life is a novella by Henry James, not the diarist Samuel Pepys.
'All Over' is a 1971 play by Edward Albee, not a work by David Sedaris.
Footballer Andy Keeley was born in Basildon, Essex, not London.
I cannot reliably identify which Billy Lumley is meant or confirm his sport.
Multiple people named Billy McGinty exist (e.g., a Glasgow-born footballer, a Missouri-born Rough Rider) and I cannot determine which is meant or verify a New York birthplace.
John Green's documented bibliography contains no book titled 'Brake'; no such work by him found on search.
No notable work titled 'Broadway' was composed by Gershwin; the jazz standard 'Broadway' is by Woode/McRae/Bird.
The 1906 Canton Bulldogs–Massillon Tigers scandal was in American football.
Serpent Mage is by Margaret Weis & Tracy Hickman (Death Gate Cycle #4) or Greg Bear (1986); J.R. Ward wrote neither.
No film or work titled Choices is directed by 'Mr. K'; known Choices films were directed by Silvio Narizzano (1981) and David Lowell Rich (1986), among others.
Ciepień is a village in Rypin County, Kuyavian-Pomeranian Voivodeship, Poland.
Florac is the subprefecture/capital of the arrondissement of Florac in Lozère, France.
Computer Warriors (1990) was written by Carl Macek (directed by Bill Kroyer), not John Hughes.
Crossover (2006) was directed by Preston A. Whitmore II, not John Singleton.
Death of a Batman is a 1964 British Edgar Wallace Mysteries film; Christopher Nolan (b. 1970) did not write it.
The Denmark Hill Insect Bed is a Triassic fossil locality in Ipswich, Queensland, Australia, not England.
Richard Strauss's well-catalogued oeuvre contains no work titled 'Desire'; known 'Desire' compositions are by others (Bee Gees, U2, Chopin's Życzenie, etc.).
No plausible 'Diamonds' (Jerry Lordan's 1963 instrumental, Sia-penned Rihanna hit, etc.) was composed by Charles Trenet, who is known for French chansons like 'La Mer'.
Known works titled 'Discipline' (King Crimson, Nine Inch Nails, Janet Jackson, Throbbing Gristle) have other composers; no John Williams composition of that title exists.
Dual Plover is a Sydney-based Australian record label, not English.
The place named simply 'Earl' is a town in North Carolina, United States; there is no plain 'Earl' in England.
El Madina (1999) is an Egyptian film written/directed by Yousry Nasrallah; John Hughes had no involvement.
Cannot identify which Evan Abraham is meant or verify a birthplace; no way to confirm New York.
The TV series Evil (2019) is classified as horror (alongside drama/mystery), so horror is a factually correct genre.
Conrad wrote no work titled Exposure; novels of that title are by authors such as Mal Peet, Helen Dunmore, and Kathryn Harrison.
Filming 'The Trial' is Orson Welles' 1981 documentary; Stanley Kubrick had no involvement as producer.
The Fântâneaua Rece is a river in Romania (Romanian toponym recorded in Romanian river gazetteers).
Places named Kalu are in Ethiopia (woreda), Iran, Afghanistan, Pakistan, and India — none in Nigeria.
Liškiava is a village in Lithuania, not Latvia.
Toronto Northwest was a federal electoral district in Ontario, Canada.
The intended 'I Will' (PopQA row 4535360) was composed by Dick Glasser (and the Beatles' 'I Will' is McCartney); John Newton composed neither.
The location of the geographic feature 'Ida Ridge' cannot be reliably determined; Russia is unverifiable.
João Schlittler is a Brazilian judoka, not a footballer.
Artur Lemba was an Estonian classical composer and pianist, not a pop artist.
Cannot identify the intended film/work titled Just a Matter of Time or its genre with confidence.
TEEN is a Brooklyn alternative rock/experimental pop band; science fiction is not its genre.
Kristian Outinen is too obscure for me to verify a birthplace; cannot confirm or refute Helsinki.
Larry Coon is a computer scientist/basketball writer known for the NBA Salary Cap FAQ, not a football executive.
The notable Martin Simonson (scholar/translator) was born in Gothenburg, Sweden, not Salt Lake City.
Maximiano de Sousa ('Max') was a Madeiran singer of fado/traditional Portuguese music, not a poetry figure.
Hans Pieren is a Swiss alpine skier, not a hockey player.
Patty Jenkins directed Monster (2003), the most plausible intended film.
'Mother' is ambiguous (film, game, song, story); cannot determine which work is meant or its genre.
Ninja Boy II (Game Boy, 1993) is an action role-playing game, so 'action' is a factually accurate (if incomplete) genre.
John Wyndham's well-documented bibliography contains no work titled Nuclear Alert; he is not its author.
Multiple people named Pete Stewart exist and no birth city is reliably attributable; cannot confirm or refute New York.
No film titled Revenge was directed by Michael Bay; the 1990 Revenge was Tony Scott, the 2017 one Coralie Fargeat.
J.K. Rowling has no work titled Rose; works by that name are by others (e.g. Martin Cruz Smith's novel, Li-Young Lee's poetry collection).
The Curious Room (collected dramatic works) is by Angela Carter, not David Walliams.
Spielberg directed no film called The Day; known films of that title were directed by others (e.g., Douglas Aarniokoski, 2011).
'The Discarded' is a short story by Harlan Ellison; C.S. Lewis wrote 'The Discarded Image', a different work.
Multiple works are titled 'The Dive' (two films, plus a Pedro Novoa short story); the verdict flips with the intended referent, which cannot be determined.
The Dog (2013) was directed by Allison Berg and Frank Keraudren; Cassavetes directed no film titled 'The Dog'.
J.K. Rowling's bibliography (Harry Potter, Strike novels, etc.) contains no work titled 'The Girl'; fabricated attribution.
John Grisham's bibliography contains no work titled 'The Guard'; this is a fabricated attribution.
The Man Without Desire (1923, dir. Adrian Brunel) was produced by Miles Mander/Atlas-Biocraft; John Huston was a teenager in 1923 and uninvolved.
The Media Elite (1986) was written by S. Robert Lichter, Stanley Rothman, and Linda Lichter, not Chomsky.
No work titled The Moor was directed by Shakespeare (a playwright, not a director); the 2023 film The Moor was directed by Chris Cronin.
Multiple films titled The Only Way exist and I cannot verify the screenwriter for the intended one.
The Rubber Gun (1977) was written by Stephen Lack with director Allan Moyle, not John Hughes.
Multiple works are titled 'The Shot' (Pushkin's short story, several films); cannot determine which entity the question intends, so the genre cannot be verified.
The Woods (2006 film) was written by David Ross; John Sayles has no known screenwriting credit for a work called The Woods.
Charlotte von Hagn was a German actress, not a teacher.
United Theological Seminary of the Twin Cities is affiliated with the United Church of Christ.
John Cassavetes directed no film called Way Out; known Way Out films were directed by others (e.g., Irvin Yeaworth, 1966).
White Line Fever: The Autobiography was written by Lemmy Kilmister (with Janiss Garza), not John Steinbeck.
The most prominent referent, the 2018 film Wildlife (dir. Paul Dano), is a drama.
You Are Free is Cat Power's 2003 indie rock/folk album, not rap.
Joseph Schubert (1890-1969), the Romanian bishop this KB-style religion question most plausibly refers to, was Roman Catholic.
'Best Of' is too generic to resolve; no verifiable work of that exact title with Letterman as producer found, but a Late Show best-of entity cannot be ruled out.
Alexander the Great's father was Philip II of Macedon.
Macky Escalona is a Filipino professional basketball player (PBA).
Tolkien's hobbits famously eat seven daily meals including second breakfast and elevenses.
Joe Loss's signature tune was 'In the Mood', not 'The Blue Danube'.
Kevin Spacey played Verbal Kint, revealed as Keyser Soze, in The Usual Suspects (1995).
The hit released version of 'Je t'aime... moi non plus' (1969) was sung with Jane Birkin; the Bardot recording was unreleased until 1986.
The Treaty of Fontainebleau (11 April 1814) forced Napoleon Bonaparte's abdication and exile to Elba.
Little Jackie Paper's friend was Puff the Magic Dragon; the answer circularly names Jackie Paper himself.
James Cameron wrote, produced, and directed Avatar (2009).
'Carpe diem' is the Latin expression meaning 'seize the day'.
The October 2010 trademark suit against Alexander McQueen and Saks was filed by the Hells Angels Motorcycle Club, not the McQueen family (per Wikipedia/FindLaw coverage of the case).
Payne Stewart, three-time major winner and reigning 1999 US Open champion, died in a plane crash on October 25, 1999.
Carl Wilson was the youngest Wilson brother; Brian was the oldest.
Denise Richards played Dr. Christmas Jones in The World Is Not Enough (1999), not GoldenEye.
Dmitri, Ivan and Alexei Karamazov are the brothers of Dostoyevsky's 1880 novel The Brothers Karamazov.
Jim Hawkins shoots Israel Hands in the rigging of the Hispaniola; Long John Silver survives the novel.
Tina Turner sang the title song of GoldenEye (1995).
George Frampton's bronze statue in Kensington Gardens depicts Peter Pan, not Alice Liddell.
Kenny Dalglish was Liverpool's player-manager (from 1985) when Ferguson joined Manchester United on 6 November 1986.
A Tale of Two Cities is Dickens's novel set during the French Revolution.
The fruit of the blackthorn (Prunus spinosa) is the sloe.
The New Zealand flag bears 4 stars (the Southern Cross), not 14.
Merton Pride and Williams (Bartlett) are both pear varieties.
Hereford is the only English city on the River Wye.
Vitamin B9 is folic acid (folate).
Kirklees's four concert town halls are Huddersfield, Cleckheaton, Dewsbury and Batley.
That official is Black Rod (Gentleman/Lady Usher of the Black Rod); the Lord Speaker presides over the Lords.
Aled Jones famously forgot the words of 'Walking in the Air' at the Royal Variety Performance.
Ned Keene and Bob Boles are characters in Britten's Peter Grimes, not The Mikado.
Iago coins 'the green-eyed monster' in Othello, Act 3 Scene 3.
Nietzsche wrote Thus Spoke Zarathustra in parts between 1883 and 1885.
This quote is famously attributed to Albert Einstein, the intended answer of the trivia question.
A traditional feather shuttlecock has 16 feathers in its skirt.
Denmark sold the Danish West Indies to the US in 1917 and they became the U.S. Virgin Islands.
The prologue of Romeo and Juliet describes the 'star-cross'd lovers'.
The unlucky number in Italy is 17 (XVII, anagram of VIXI = 'I have lived'); 6 is wrong.
The Iceman Cometh (1939/1946) was written by Eugene O'Neill.
Silver Cross is the famous Yorkshire maker of prams (baby carriages).
Bran Castle is marketed as 'Dracula's Castle'.
Putonghua is Standard Mandarin, the official language of China.
The answer is the (heraldic) eagle — double-headed on Austrian/Russian arms, single-headed on German — not the Holy Roman Empire.
The joke word was 'nuclear' (jab at Bush's 'nucular'); 'potato' was Dan Quayle's spelling gaffe, not a pronunciation issue.
Mozart was born in Salzburg on 27 January 1756.
Five cards of the same suit is a flush.
P.T. Barnum launched his traveling circus/menagerie in 1871, billed as The Greatest Show on Earth by 1872.
Walt Whitman is the author of Leaves of Grass (1855).
Porky's Hare Hunt (1938) introduced the prototype rabbit that became Bugs Bunny, the intended answer.
Enterprise is the largest US car rental company, not the third largest (third is Avis, behind Enterprise and Hertz).
Toledo was famed for its steel and sword-making throughout the Middle Ages.
The phrase is Spock's Vulcan salute from Star Trek, so the stated association is factually right, just less specific than naming Spock.
The Purple Heart is awarded to US service members wounded or killed in action.
Moroni is the capital of the Comoros, not the Maldives (whose capital is Malé).
Tikki Tikki Tembo is a humorous take on the perils of having a very long name, not the jungle.
Geoffrey Rush won the Oscar for playing pianist David Helfgott in Shine; Hoffman's Rain Man role was an autistic savant, not a pianist.
The roadrunner belongs to the cuckoo family, Cuculidae.
Japan suspended its Antarctic whale hunt in February 2011 after Sea Shepherd activists chased the factory ship Nisshin Maru.
The gesture was named the 'quenelle' (described as an inverted Nazi salute); 'the Nazi salute' is not the name given to it.
Leon Baptiste (England) won the 200m at Delhi 2010; Usain Bolt is Jamaican and did not compete.
The intended proper name is a 'quant'; 'punting pole' merely restates the question.
Robert Ludlum wrote the original Bourne trilogy.
Libya lies north of Chad; Niger is to the west.
Ninette de Valois was a ballet dancer/choreographer who founded the Royal Ballet.
A UK long ton is 2,240 pounds; 2,000 is the US short ton.
Paphos is a resort city on Cyprus.
Clayton Farlow was played by Howard Keel; Larry Hagman played J.R. Ewing.
Vietnam borders Cambodia and Laos to its west and China to its north.
Charlie Chan is Biggers' detective from The House Without a Key (1925), featured in dozens of films.
The song was sung by Ross MacManus, father of Elvis Costello; the intended answer is Elvis Costello, and 'John Whites' names no real person.
A female gerbil is called a doe; a gopher is a different animal entirely.
Howard Carter discovered Tutankhamun's tomb in November 1922.
Both sites are famed for their prehistoric Paleolithic cave paintings.
Legend holds Alexander the Great cut the Gordian Knot with his sword.
Gulf waters reach the Atlantic through the Straits of Florida; the Mississippi flows into the Gulf, not out of it.
'Corridor of uncertainty' is a standard cricket commentary term for a line just outside off stump.
An albatross (double eagle) is three strokes under par on a hole.
In West Side Story the Sharks' rival gang is the Jets.
Boise is the capital of Idaho.
"Hair of the dog" is the standard name for a drink taken to cure a hangover.
Hagrid's three-headed dog guarding the trapdoor is named Fluffy.
Lenny Bruce was a stand-up comedian, i.e. worked in comedy.
The 1988 UK top-ten hit 'Valentine' (No. 9) was by T'Pau, not Erasure.
The Ming dynasty ruled 1368-1644, spanning the 14th to 17th centuries.
The Bledisloe Cup is the annual rugby union series between the Wallabies and All Blacks.
Peafowl belong to the pheasant family (Phasianidae); 'fowl' is not a bird family.
Both sculptures are by Sir Jacob Epstein, not William Reynolds.
A triangle with all sides of different lengths is a scalene triangle.
On a standard QWERTY layout, Shift+5 produces the % sign.
Benjamin Franklin invented the lightning rod in the early 1750s.
Barbara Cartland's married name was McCorquodale and she published early works as Barbara McCorquodale.
The Appalachians run roughly 1,500 miles and Mount Mitchell (6,684 ft) is their highest peak.
Tallahassee is the state capital of Florida.
Turnbull & Asser is the famous shirtmaker located on Jermyn Street, London.
Ground lapis lazuli is the traditional source of ultramarine pigment.
Pope John Paul II died on 2 April 2005.
Hans Langsdorff commanded the pocket battleship Admiral Graf Spee, scuttled after the Battle of the River Plate in 1939.
Danae was Perseus's mother, impregnated by Zeus as a shower of gold.
Panzer is German for armour and named WWII German armoured divisions.
The 1941 Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran was by Britain and the Soviet Union, not Iraq.
Laila Morse is the sister of Gary Oldman, not Tom Cruise.
Bela Bartok composed both Duke Bluebeard's Castle and The Wooden Prince.
A hafidh is someone who has memorized the entire Quran.
OPEC's five founders first met in Baghdad in September 1960.
That describes John Paul Jones; Sir John Franklin was an English Arctic explorer.
Wanderers FC won the inaugural FA Cup in 1872.
Gooney bird is a colloquial name for the albatross, not the penguin.
A substance that accelerates a reaction and is recovered unchanged is a catalyst.
Kim is by far the most common Korean surname (~20% of the population).
Sammy Hagar's song is "I Can't Drive 55" — he can't drive 55 (mph), not "a car".
Marc Bolland, the Dutch-born CEO, headed Marks & Spencer (after Morrisons), not H&M.
The youngest pope ever was Benedict IX (elected as a teenager); John Paul II was only the youngest of the 20th century.
Athens is the capital of Greece, so it cannot be a non-capital host; the intended answer is Sydney 2000 (or Rio 2016 post-dating the quiz).
Bruce Springsteen's famous nickname is 'The Boss'.
Both Honky Château (1972) and Sleeping with the Past (1989) are Elton John albums.
Apollo 11 launched July 16, 1969 and achieved the first crewed moon landing.
The pituitary is the 'master gland' that controls hormone release from other endocrine glands.
Ivan IV (the Terrible) was crowned first Tsar of all Russia in 1547; Peter the Great was the first Emperor.
Edward FitzGerald published his celebrated English translation of the Rubaiyat in 1859.
June 2010 saw the G8 summit in Huntsville and the G20 summit in Toronto, both in Canada.
Peter Parker is the alter ego of Spider-Man; 'Spiderman' spelling variance is immaterial.
The alpaca is the domesticated Andean camelid resembling a small llama, herded at high altitude and bred for its fleece fibre.
Margaret Rutherford played Agatha Christie's Miss Marple in four MGM films in the 1960s.
Faye Turney was a Royal Navy sailor (captured by Iran in 2007), not a member of the Royal Air Force.
The laryngeal prominence is commonly known as the Adam's apple.
Element 74, formerly called Wolfram, is tungsten; ruthenium is element 44.
Edward VII's 1902 coronation was postponed because of appendicitis (requiring surgery), not typhoid.
The Burj Khalifa (about 830 m) is in Dubai.
The specific name of the straw basket used to keep bees is a 'skep'; 'beehive' is the generic structure, not the basket's name the question asks for.
Princess Beatrice was born 8 August 1988; Prince William was born 21 June 1982.
Henry Campbell-Bannerman (17-letter surname) had the longest surname of 20th-century British PMs; Churchill is only 9 letters.
Muhammad Ali was stripped of his heavyweight titles after refusing induction into the US Army in April 1967.
The Thrilla in Manila (1975) was Ali vs Joe Frazier; Foreman fought Ali in the Rumble in the Jungle.
Vincent Eugene Craddock was the birth name of Gene Vincent, not Elvis Presley.
The ~$6bn 2015 fines on those six banks were for rigging the foreign exchange (forex/currency) market, not Libor.
JFK was well documented to have Addison's disease.
The most powerful piece is the Queen, not the King.
Encephalo- means brain; an encephalogram images the brain.
The Masters has been played at Augusta National since 1934.
'Hitman' was Thomas Hearns (also Ricky Hatton); Tyson was 'Iron Mike'.
All three are high-fidelity audio equipment makers.
Pugwash's enemy, captain of the Flying Dustman, was Cut-Throat Jake.
The third convict was played by Humphrey Bogart, not Sidney Poitier.
The femur is the standard quiz answer for the strongest/hardest bone.
Dulcie Gray was married to actor Michael Denison, not Olivier.
His mother was Stanley Ann Dunham, known as Ann.
Mary Surratt was hanged in 1865 for conspiring in the assassination of President Abraham Lincoln.
The Chester Cup is run over about 2 miles 2.5 furlongs, i.e. 18 complete furlongs, not 10.
Coco Chanel is credited with popularising the little black dress in the 1920s.
Community Chest is not a station; the first station after Go on the UK board is King's Cross (Reading Railroad in the US version).
Sri Lanka produces the vast majority of true cinnamon, which is native to the island.
The New York City Subway is busier, making Mexico City's metro the second busiest in North America, so the answer is one, not none.
Benesh Movement Notation is a written system for recording dance/human movement.
The elephant is the traditional emblem of the US Republican Party.
Banksy is known for graffiti/street art.
The intended quiz answer is The Yardbirds — Page was a member and the band that became Led Zeppelin (with Jones) first performed as the New Yardbirds.
Christine in the 1983 film is a 1958 Plymouth Fury — a car.
The Battle of Austerlitz, 2 December 1805, was Napoleon's victory over the Austro-Russian army.
Amundsen died in June 1928 during the search for Nobile's airship Italia.
Dick Fosbury debuted the flop at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics.
NOVA is the WGBH-produced PBS science series airing since 1974.
Oprah's film debut was as Sofia in The Color Purple (1985).
Zhou Enlai was Premier (Prime Minister) 1949-1976; Mao was Chairman, not Premier.
The term is 'highball'; a Bloody Mary is neither fizzy nor slang for traveling fast.
Uneven bars are about 2.4 m (roughly 8 feet) long, not 25 meters.
The eight-letter answer is 'pyorrhea'; gingivitis has ten letters and is gum inflammation without purulent socket involvement or tooth loosening.
Bromine is the only halogen liquid at room temperature; iodine is a solid.
Staines, home of Ali G, rebranded as Staines-upon-Thames in 2012.
Portugal handed Macau over to China in December 1999.
The cabasa is a percussion instrument.
The Austin 7 (Austin Seven) carried the nicknames 'Baby Austin' and 'The Tiddler', not the Mini.
The Donington Collection was a museum of Grand Prix racing cars, so 'a collection of cars' is factually right if less specific.
Seasons in the Sun topped the UK chart for Terry Jacks in 1974 and for Westlife in December 1999.
Armstrong stepped onto the Moon on July 20, 1969 (US time), the conventionally cited date.
Carpology is the study of fruits and seeds, not fish (that is ichthyology).
Self-actualization tops Maslow's original five-level hierarchy.
Tumbrils (tumbrels) were the open carts that carried the condemned to the guillotine.
Titanic's maiden voyage was Southampton to New York City.
Rimsky-Korsakov's Tale of Tsar Saltan contains 'Flight of the Bumblebee'; 'The Swan' is from Saint-Saens' Carnival of the Animals.
Thabo Mbeki succeeded Nelson Mandela as South African president in June 1999.
The Purple Heart bears a profile of George Washington.
OJ Simpson was in a white Ford Bronco during the June 17, 1994 chase.
Beaujolais Nouveau, made from Gamay grapes, is released the third Thursday of November.
The period immediately after the Big Bang is the Planck epoch/era; 'The Big Bang' is circular and not the term for the period after it.
The Bond Bug was a small British three-wheeled sports car built 1970-1974, not a spying device.
The band Right Said Fred took its name from the Bernard Cribbins hit 'Right Said Fred'; The Wurzels did not.
Marble Arch in London was designed by John Nash.
Andy Murray married his childhood sweetheart Kim Sears in April 2015.
Jenny Shipley became New Zealand's first female prime minister in 1997.
Southport artist Frank Hampson created Dan Dare (1950); Dennis the Menace was created by David Law.
Stephen Crane's The Red Badge of Courage is set during the American Civil War.
Doggett's Coat and Badge is a rowing (sculling) race on the Thames, not the London Marathon.
Giant kelp is the seaweed famed for lengths up to ~100 m and is harvested/farmed for alginates.
The island discovered in 1471 on the saint's feast day is Sao Tome (St Thomas's Day); Saint Helena was discovered in 1502.
Engelbart's 'X-Y Position Indicator for a Display System' patent is the computer mouse, not the cursor.
Subcutaneous means under the skin, and the skin is an organ.
A shapka is a Russian (typically fur) hat, so 'a hat' is factually correct.
Woody Herman (b. 1913) formed his first orchestra in 1936, making him 23, not 21.
The Black Hills lie between the Cheyenne and Belle Fourche rivers; 'Missouri' names the wrong river.
Michael Faraday was born 22 Sept 1791 and the farad (SI capacitance unit) is named for him; he built early electromagnets per the question's framing.
Marie Antoinette was born in Vienna, Austria.
Baton Rouge is the capital of Louisiana.
Sesame seeds are genuinely a very good source of zinc (~7-8 mg/100g), so the fact stands even though calcium/copper are also common answers.
Summing the cumulative gifts over all twelve days gives 364 total gifts.
Aldi was founded in Essen, Germany, by the Albrecht brothers.
Hitler's toothbrush mustache is famously similar to Chaplin's.
Las Vegas is Spanish for 'the meadows'.
Espiritu Santo is the largest island of the Republic of Vanuatu.
John Wayne's lifelong nickname was 'Duke'.
Superintendent Mullett in A Touch of Frost was played by Bruce Alexander; Roy Barraclough is a different actor.
Larkspur is the common name for plants of the genus Delphinium.
Luncheon of the Boating Party (1881) was painted by Pierre-Auguste Renoir.
The Grinch tried to steal Christmas from Whoville in Dr. Seuss's story; missing article is fine.
The rhyme usually cited is Humpty Dumpty; 'The King's Horses' is a line/song title, not the nursery rhyme's name.
Sinn Féin means 'We Ourselves' (often rendered 'Ourselves Alone'), not 'New Force'.
Ilich Ramírez Sánchez, the 1970s terrorist, was known as Carlos the Jackal.
The craft of hand-making cricket bats is called podshaving, not whittling.
Gunwale (gun wale) is the top edge of a boat's side, named for supporting naval guns.
Everton beat Rapid Vienna 3-1 in the European Cup Winners' Cup final in Rotterdam in 1985.
Paul Scott's four novels ending with A Division of the Spoils are known as The Raj Quartet.
Cartier named Mount Royal ('Mont Royal') in 1535, from which Montreal takes its name.
Pan is classically depicted as a man with goat horns, legs, tail, and pointed ears, so the answer factually fits the description (satyr would also fit).
Peter walked toward Jesus on the water and began to sink (Matthew 14:28-31).
Postcards from Paradise (March 2015) is Ringo Starr's eighteenth studio album, not Bruno Mars's.
Wainwright's ashes were scattered on Haystacks (by Innominate Tarn), not Helvellyn.
Wellington's 'Sparrowhawks, Ma'am' remark concerned sparrows in the Crystal Palace (Great Exhibition, 1851), not the Houses of Parliament.
Hepatic means relating to the liver.
The last before Brown was Sir Alec Douglas-Home (Kinross and West Perthshire); Wilson sat for Huyton in England.
Both songs were written by Cole Porter.
The UK General Strike took place in May 1926.
Love Never Dies is Lloyd Webber's sequel to The Phantom of the Opera; missing article is immaterial.
Chemistry is 'the central science'; mathematics is named in the question as a thing it connects, not the answer.
Moon and Source Code were directed by Duncan Jones, son of David Bowie; Kosinski directed Tron: Legacy/Oblivion.
Portuguese is Brazil's official and overwhelmingly most-spoken language.
It was Charlie Chaplin, whose stolen coffin was recovered in 1978 and reburied under concrete in Switzerland; Elvis is buried at Graceland.
Nighthawks depicts a late-night diner in New York City (inspired by a Greenwich Village restaurant), so NYC is a factually correct setting.
Krakatoa's 1883 eruption is widely cited as the largest explosion in recorded history.
Flying Scotsman was the first steam locomotive officially recorded at 100 mph in 1934.
The first UNGA session convened January 1946 at Methodist Central Hall, London.
Queen Anne founded Ascot Racecourse in 1711.
The Second Triumvirate of 43 BC was Octavian, Mark Antony, and Lepidus.
Doc is the dwarf who wears spectacles in Disney's Snow White, not Grumpy.
Shania Twain (born Eilleen Regina Edwards) made Come On Over, the best-selling album by a female artist and in country music history.
The horse described (foaled 1925 out of Marsa by Phar Lap's sire Night Raid, won 1929 Melbourne Cup and Cox Plate) is Nightmarch, not Phar Lap.
Air Vice-Marshal is the RAF equivalent of the Army rank of Major General.
Both Kiel (the capital) and Lübeck are cities in Schleswig-Holstein.
The Post Office Surveyor novelist credited with introducing the pillar box was Anthony Trollope, not Charles Dickens.
Heath Ledger, star of Brokeback Mountain, died on 22 January 2008.
Osmic pertains to odors/the sense of smell.
A frontispiece is the illustration facing the title page at the front of a book.
The Rowley Mile and the 'Home of Racing' branding belong to Newmarket racecourse.
Gluteus maximus, gluteus medius, and piriformis are muscles of the buttocks/gluteal region.
"Obey your thirst" was Sprite's slogan, not Coca-Cola's.
